shows.name: Love's Labour's Lost
shows.description_short: This blazing, flamenco‑inspired production of Shakespeare’s comedy of love, wit and broken vows, sets the Globe’s stage alight.
shows.description_full:
Get ready for a sizzling Shakespeare summer in London with this brand new production of Love’s Labour’s Lost at Shakespeare’s Globe on Bankside. This fiery reimagining of one of Shakespeare’s lesser-known plays is a flamenco‑infused production, in which vows of study are undone by passion, poetry, and dancing feet. Experience Love Labour’s Lost as you’ve never seen it before, alight in rhythm and romance - secure your tickets today!

In sun‑drenched Navarre, four noble lords swear off love and all worldly pleasures in pursuit of study and self‑discipline. Their plans are joyously derailed when the Princess of France and her spirited entourage arrive on a diplomatic mission, bringing wit, music, and irresistible allure.

This brand new production of Shakespeare's playful, lyrical comedy infuses this rarely staged gem with the vibrant spirit of flamenco in a tale of oaths, desires, and the inevitable havoc love wreaks on even the most resolved hearts.

Written in the late 1590s, Love’s Labour’s Lost is celebrated for its energetic wordplay, intricate rhymes, and youthful wit. Though less frequently staged than Shakespeare’s greatest hits, the play has enjoyed notable productions over the centuries, including a 2009 Globe staging directed by former Globe Artistic Director Dominic Dromgoole.

This summer’s production embraces that rich tradition while offering a bold contemporary twist, inviting audiences to experience Shakespeare’s joyous celebration of language, love and the human heart in one of the world’s most iconic theatrical spaces.

Casting for this production is to be announced.

Love’s Labour’s Lost is written by William Shakespeare.

This production is directed by Indiana Lown‑Collins ( The Solid Life of Sugar Water ), making her Globe debut following her time as a Resident Artist and JMK Award winner.

Set and costume design is by Katie Lias ( Much Ado About Nothing ), with movement direction from Carmen Igarza ( Manolita Chen ), infusing the production with flamenco-inspired physicality.

Summer 2026 is heating up with this bold new production, a fiery, unstoppably joyful ride into the heart of the Bard’s back catalogue! Rarely seen on UK stages compared with his more famous works, Love’s Labour’s Lost is a raw and refreshing exploration of connection and the price of desire. Book your tickets for Love’s Labour’s Lost at Shakespeare’s Globe now!

shows.name: Dog Man: The Musical
shows.description_short: Crime-biting capers and comic book chaos await in this hilarious musical adventure for kids! Best for age 5+.
shows.typical_age_range: Best for age 5+. Children under 16 must be accompanied by and sat next to an adult.
shows.description_full:
Get ready for tail-wagging fun as Dog Man: The Musical bounds into the Queen Elizabeth Hall, Southbank Centre, on scenic Bankside, for a limited run. This high-energy kids' musical is based on the bestselling book series by Dav Pilkey, creator of Captain Underpants and Cat Kid Comic Club , and tells the story of a half-dog, half-human superhero. With quirky characters, catchy songs, and chaotic comedy, this “fun, goofy musical romp” (Broadway World) is perfect for families this summer holidays and makes its UK debut following an acclaimed off-Broadway run in 2024.

Best friends and comic-book masterminds George and Harold are back - and this time, they’ve gone full razzle dazzle. The ten-year-old pranksters decide it’s time to bring their most beloved character to life: Dog Man, the fearless, furniture-chewing crime-fighter who’s part canine, part cop, and all hero. And this isn't just any old Dog Man story - it’s a musical. But things quickly get ruff for this crime-biting hero. Dog Man’s arch-nemesis, Petey the Cat, has cloned himself for double trouble, the villainous cyborg fish Flippy is building an army of Beasty Buildings, and George and Harold might not finish their show before lunchtime! Bursting with silly humour, giant puppets, cartoon action and comic book-style staging, Dog Man: The Musical captures the anarchic charm of cartoonist and author Dav Pilkey’s books while encouraging kids to embrace imagination and teamwork.

Dog Man: The Musical is based on the hugely successful children’s graphic novels by Dav Pilkey, whose irreverent humour and doodle-style drawings have won him millions of fans around the world. First published in 2016, the Dog Man books have sold over 50 million copies and are known for combining slapstick silliness with messages of kindness, creativity and second chances. The musical premiered Off-Broadway in 2019, presented by TheatreWorksUSA, and was praised by The New York Times as “a hilarious delight” that successfully brought the chaos and heart of the books to life. It has since toured the US and enjoyed widespread acclaim from families and educators alike. Fun fact: Dav Pilkey came up with the idea for Dog Man when he was just a kid himself, as the character was invented during a school assignment that resulted in him being sent to the hallway for disrupting class! That same spirit of rebellious creativity now fuels this hilarious musical.

Casting for this production is to be announced.

The musical is directed and choreographed by Jen Wineman ( The 25th Annual Putnam County Spelling Bee, Shakespeare in Love ) with book and lyrics by Emmy nominee and Tony winner Kevin Del Aguila ( Peg + Cat, Altar Boyz ), and music by Brad Alexander ( Clifford the Big Red Dog, Click, Clack, Moo ). The show is produced by TheatreWorksUSA, a company known for creating fantastic theatre experiences for younger audiences. Further creative team members are to be announced.

Join the tail-wagging, villain-thwarting fun as Dog Man: The Musical brings the pages of your kids’ favourite book to life! With larger-than-life puppetry, jokes for all ages, big-hearted songs, and heroic hijinks, it’s a paws-itively perfect pick for school holidays. Book your tickets for Dog Man: The Musical at the Queen Elizabeth Hall today, and get ready for laughs, licks, and lots of live-action comic book musical magic!

shows.name: The Children
shows.description_short: Meera Syal leads this 10th anniversary revival of Lucy Kirkwood’s drama about environmental catastrophy and generational responsibility.
shows.typical_age_range: Best for age 14+. Children under 16 must be accompanied by and sat next to an adult.
shows.description_full:
National treasure Meera Syal leads the cast of The Children , coming to the Lyric Hammersmith near St Paul’s Green. Lucy Kirkwood’s Tony-nominated play about a married couple living in the aftermath of a nuclear disaster, whose life is upended by a figure from their past, celebrates it’s 10th anniversary in this major new revival. Darkly funny and deeply gripping, this “ingenious” (Guardian) drama “asks profound questions” (Guardian) about legacy, the decision to have children and environmental disaster. Secure your tickets today.

In the aftermath of a nuclear disaster, married couple Hazel and Robin are trying to cling to normality, despite living on the edge of a radiation exclusion zone. Hazel has her yoga and stays in contact with their eldest child, Robin farms and brews parsnip wine - so what if electricity is rationed and a geiger counter is on hand at all times?

When their old friend and former colleague, Rose, a nuclear physicist, knocks on their door, their attempt at normality soon crumbles. Rose brings with her buried secrets and a startling proposition that will test the couple’s loyalty and cause them to reassess what they owe to the next generation.

The Children premiered at the Royal Court in 2016 before transferring to Broadway in 2017, receiving two Tony nominations. It has since toured internationally and been revived in the UK. In 2019, The Guardian ranked The Children third on a list of the greatest theatrical works created post-2000. The show returns to London this September in celebration of its 10th anniversary.

Dame Meera Syal stars as Hazel. Syal is an Olivier-nominated comedian, playwright, novelist, journalist and actress, best known for her roles in The Kumars at No. 42 , Goodness Gracious Me and Anita and Me . She holds a CBE, a DBE, a BAFTA Fellowship, and is a Fellow of the Royal Society of Literature. Her stage credits include Shirley Valentine, Behind the Beautiful Forevers, Annie, Noises Off , and Much Ado About Nothing .

Further casting is to be announced.

The Children is by Lucy Kirkwood. Kirkwood is a playwright and screenwriter, best known for her plays Chimerica, Mosquitoes, NSFW and The Welkin . She is a Tony nominee and an Olivier, Critics’ Circle Theatre Award and Evening Standard Theatre Award winner.

Lyric Artistic Director Rachel O’Riordan directs. O’Riordan's credits include The Weir, Iphigenia in Splott, A Doll's House, Everything is Illuminated and Killology. In 2017, O’Riordan was named one of The Stage newspaper’s 100 most influential people in theatre in the UK.

This award-winning play is more prescient than ever. Starring Meera Syal, this bitingly witty “genuinely disturbing” (Guardian) drama dissects issues of generational responsibility, personal accountability and environmental crisis. Lucy Kirkwood's “taut tale of human and atomic meltdown” (Guardian) is unmissable - secure your tickets for The Children at the Lyric Hammersmith today.

shows.name: Jeeves Takes Charge
shows.description_short: Sam Harrison gives a tour-de-force performance in this one-man adaptation of P.G. Wodehouse's beloved Jeeves and Wooster stories.
shows.description_full:
First seen 45 years ago, the unexpected theatrical phenomenon, Jeeves Takes Charge , returns to the stage for a limited season this summer. Playing at the Charing Cross Theatre, close to The Strand, this comedy play is an “absolute treat” (Bath Echo) for anyone wanting to experience P. G. Wodehouse’s gloriously absurd world. Brought to life in a tour-de-force performance by Sam Harrison - secure your tickets today!

Adapted by James Duke, Edward Duke and Hugh Woolridge, Jeeves Takes Charge transports us to a world of country houses, terrifying aunts, engagements gone wrong and gentlemanly panic. Based on a short story by P.G. Wodehouse published in 1916, meet the unfortunate Bertie Wooster, who is steered away from disaster – and an engagement to Lady Florence Craye – at every turn by the brilliantly resourceful valet Jeeves.

Jeeves Takes Charge first debuted in London in 1980 by Barbara Dickson and Bernard Theobald with Andrew Lloyd Webber and Cameron Mackintosh. It earned an Olivier Award for Edward Duke’s performance as Most Promising Newcomer. There was a subsequent production at the Lyric Theatre, Hammersmith.

The latest production, which also starred Sam Harrison, opened at the Theatre Royal Bath before acclaimed runs at the Jermyn Street Theatre and Tabard Theatre. This new staging at the Charing Cross Theatre, for a limited time, will precede a UK tour in 2027.

Sam Harrison brings to life twenty-two characters in this one-man adaptation show, heading to London’s Charing Cross Theatre. He is a West End, Chichester and RSC favourite with credits including The Magician’s Elephant , Les Miserables and Chitty Chitty Bang Bang .

Hugh Woolridge directs the production. His directing credits include Chess in Concert, Sondheim Tonight , and the last staging of Jeeves Takes Charge at Theatre Royal Bath in 2024.

The rest of the creative team includes scenic design by Caite Hevner, lighting design by Tim Mitchell, and sound design by Paul Groothuis.

This summer, take a trip to experience the warmth, silliness and sheer brilliance of P. G. Wodehouse’s writing during this limited run in the heart of London. This superb Olivier Award-winning comedy is not to be missed — book your tickets for Jeeves Takes Charge at the Charing Cross Theatre today!

shows.name: Brainiac Live!
shows.description_short: The Olivier Award-winning, explosive family show heads to the Garrick Theatre. Best for age 5+.
shows.typical_age_range: Best for age 5+. Children under 16 must be accompanied by and sat next to an adult.
shows.description_full:
The Brainiacs are set to blow your mind this summer with the return of their most popular and explosive live family science show! Based on the popular UK television show that aired on Sky One in 2003, Brainiac Live makes science thrilling for audiences of all ages at the Garrick Theatre, located just a stone’s throw away from the bustling area of Leicester Square and Chinatown.

Expected to be more mischievous than ever, Brainiac Live takes all those science experiments that are deemed too dangerous to try at home and brings them to life on the West End stage. From exploding microwaves, vortex smoke rings and exploding hydrogen balloons, to supercharged spinning chairs, Brainiac Live is a “60-minute entertainment spectacle that thrills with every crash, bang and wallop” (LondonTheatre1).

Brainiac Live has been a firm favourite for families since its first staging in 2008, and it has gone on to become an international success. The show’s West End debut took place at the Palace Theatre, current home of Harry Potter and the Cursed Child , in 2013. It went on to have more summer runs in London, as well as a two-week season at the Sydney Opera House and other subsequent productions in places like Hong Kong and Barcelona. Brainiac Live has also headlined major science festivals, and a recent dual-language English/Arabic production was staged in Saudi Arabia.

Last year, its success was rewarded when the show won the 2025 Olivier Award for Best Family show.

The 2026 West End company will be announced soon.

Brainiac Live is produced by Dan Colman. Other creatives on the team include Andy Joyce as creative director and Damian Stanton as designer.

Experience all the weird and wonderful world of science from the safety of your seat as Brainiac Live arrives in London with a bang at the Garrick Theatre for a limited run. This “show is quite literally, explosive, in both its energy and experiments” (Spy in the Stalls), making it the ideal addition to your plans this summer – don't miss it!

shows.name: The Afronauts
shows.description_short: Zambia, 1964; a schoolteacher rallies misfit dreamers to train as astronauts and defy the space race’s status quo.
shows.description_full:
The Afronauts rockets into London for its world premiere. This unmissable new play about resistance, imagination, and reaching for the stars from Olivier-nominated playwright Ryan Calais Cameron reimagines a little-known chapter of African history. Don't miss this sharply witty, politically charged play, coming to the Royal Court Theatre, in vibrant Sloane Square, for a strictly limited run in winter 2026, commissioned especially as part of the theatre’s 70th anniversary season.

1964: As the USA and USSR battle it out to reach the moon, another contender enters the space race - Zambia. In a country that has just gained its independence, one schoolteacher gathers a band of eccentric students and launches them into a dream that defies gravity.

With limited resources and limitless ambition, he gathers a group of ambitious misfits for the ultimate mission. What follows is part sci-fi fable, part political satire, part celebration of a nation reaching beyond the colonial legacy.

The Afronauts is inspired by the true(ish) story of Zambia’s 1964 space programme, reimagined by award winning playwright Ryan Calais Cameron. This world premiere is supported by the Genesis Foundation and is the first commission from their new Royal Court programme, designed to support bold, original work.

Casting for The Afronauts is to be announced in five, four, three, two…

The Afronauts is written by Ryan Calais Cameron. Calais Cameron is a Black British Theatre and Sky Arts Theatre Award winner and Olivier nominee. His credits include For Black Boys Who Have Considered Suicide When the Hue Gets Too Heavy, Queens of Sheba, Human Nurture, and Retrograde as well as Boarders for the BBC and Queenie for Channel 4.

Further creative team members are to be announced.

If you’re looking for theatre that rockets beyond the ordinary, The Afronauts offers a story of dreaming big when history told you to stay small. Don't miss the premiere of this bold new work from one of the UK’s most exciting young playwrights. Ambition becomes resistance, and the cosmos becomes a battleground for identity, legacy, and freedom - it promises to be out of this world. Book tickets for The Afronauts at the Royal Court Theatre today.

shows.name: The Gruffalo
shows.description_short: Introduce your children to the magical world of live theatre as their favourite picture book bursts onto the stage! Best for age 3+.
shows.typical_age_range: Best for age 3+. Babes in arms under 18 months. Under 16s must be accompanied and seated next to an adult.
shows.description_full:
Take a stroll into the deep dark theatre and discover… The Gruffalo ! Celebrate 25 years of Julia Donaldson and Axel Scheffler’s beloved picture book with this boisterous stage adaptation from Tall Stories. The much-loved show has delighted families around the world and now returns to the West End’s Lyric Theatre, just a short walk from Piccadilly Circus, for another summer season.

Join Mouse as she heads into the woods searching for hazelnuts. Along the way, she runs into the eccentric Owl, cunning Fox and sneaky Snake. They’re all hungry and Mouse is the perfect size for their dinner plates! Quick-thinking Mouse distracts them with a tale about the fearsome Gruffalo… but is he just a product of her imagination, or is he real?

The Gruffalo has delivered just the right amount of scares for 25 years and is the most beloved of former Children’s Laureate Julia Donaldson’s creations. With its instantly recognisable illustrations from Axel Scheffler, this award-winning picture book is a modern classic, featuring an eclectic cast of woodland creatures and a small but plucky protagonist. Millions of children have enjoyed it read aloud as a bedtime story or seen it on the television - but this musical adaptation brings it to life in a whole new way!

Casting for this production is to be announced.

Kid’s theatre company Tall Stories have adapted several classic picture books for the stage, including Elmer and Room on the Broom , but none are quite as beloved as Donaldson’s not-so-fearsome monster. The Gruffalo is packed with songs, laughs, and physical theatre to get the whole family cheering as Mouse’s daring adventure unfolds! Even better, at 60 minutes it is the perfect length for little ones who are watching a theatre show for the very first time.

This magical story ticks all the right boxes for an unforgettable day out. Get ready for monstrous fun in the West End! If you are looking for the best show to make your toddler or pre-schooler love the theatre, book tickets for The Gruffalo at the Lyric Theatre today.

shows.name: La Traviata
shows.description_short: Verdi’s heartbreaking story of love and sacrifice is stripped back to its core with the return of Peter Konwitschny’s production.
shows.description_full:
Passion, music and heartbreak collide in Peter Konwitschny’s operatic masterpiece of Verdi’s iconic romantic tragedy, La Traviata. Playing at the London Coliseum, a short walk from the National Gallery, this is the perfect production for those discovering opera for the first time, whilst still offering a rich experience for seasoned enthusiasts of this art form. Secure your tickets for this unmissable opera today!

La Traviata shares the story of Violetta Valéry, a celebrated Parisian courtesan who returns to high society after an extended period of illness and discovers love with the earnest Alfredo Germont. However, they are blighted by societal pressures and personal sacrifice.

Combined with some of Verdi’s instantly recognisable melodies, such as "Let me live the life I want to (Sempre Libera)" and "Brindisi Let’s drink and be merry (Libiamo)", La Traviata is a heartbreaking drama that explores love, freedom, class and mortality.

Written in 1853, Verdi’s opera made its premiere at the La Fenice opera house in Venice later that year. Although it was initially unpopular with audiences, now it is one of the best-known works and one of the most recognisable operas, with many versions of the story being presented on stages across the world.

Recognised for having a more modernistic, minimalist lens on this story, Peter Konwitschny’s production earned an Olivier Award for Best New Opera following its premiere in 2013. “A radical reimagination of La Traviata that bursts with possibility” (Oscar Cunningham), a second revival by director Ruth Knight cemented this opera as a firm favourite with audiences.

The cast of this production may vary depending on the performance date. Principal cast includes:

PJ Harris will direct the production with Kevin John Edusei as the conductor.

Hailed as being radical, captivating and powerful and an exciting reinvigoration of a classic by critics, there’s no better time to experience one of the most powerful operas for the first time in the heart of London. Book your tickets to see La Traviata at the London Coliseum for a culturally rich experience in 2027!

shows.name: Relics
shows.description_short: Four siblings reunite to clear out their late mother’s house in this darkly comic drama about loss, legacy and learning who you are.
shows.description_full:
Relics, a dark new family drama all about the legacies we inherit, and the choices and compromises we make growing up, makes its world premiere at West London’s vibrant Lyric Hammersmith. This bold new play from up-and-coming playwright Ben Ockrent, directed by the legendary Michael Longhurst, is heartbreaking and hilarious in turn. Don't miss out - book your tickets to Relics t oday!

After the death of their mother, four siblings must come home to sift through the detritus of her life. They include bossy, bulldozing Liv, who constantly micromanages her siblings, people-pleaser Rob, who just wants them all to just get along for once, and the fed-up Michelle, who is sick of playing nice and walking on eggshells around her family's fragile egos. And when the prodigal son, Jonny, unexpectedly returns, he seems more determined than ever to push everyone’s buttons.

Though the siblings return to their family home with the intention of dividing up their mother’s belongings, what they unearth there will challenge everything they thought they knew about themselves and one another. As explosive secrets come to light, the family are set to lose everything - including their dignity.

This production marks the play's world premiere. Secure your tickets today!

Casting for Relics is to be announced.

Relics is written by Ben Ockrent. Ockrent is an award-winning playwright, director and screenwriter. His credits include Hacktivists for National Theatre Connections, Carrot for Theatre 503, At Risk and short play Honey , commissioned as part of Olivier-nominated short play series The Great Game , both at the Kiln Theatre (formerly the Tricycle), as well as screenwriting on Waterloo Road and Material Girls for BBC1 and Youngers for E4.

Michael Longhurst directs. Longhurst is an Olivier-nominated director and former Artistic Director of the Donmar Warehouse. His credits for the Donmar Warehouse include Constellations , Blindness, The Cherry Orchard, Macbeth starring David Tennant and Cush Jumbo and the UK premiere of Pulitzer-winning musical Next to Normal . Other credits include Caroline, or Change for the Chichester Festival Theatre, Amadeus for the National Theatre and Much Ado about Nothing for the RSC.

Relics is the riotously funny and deeply moving new play from rising star playwright Ben Ockrent, directed by Michael Longhurst. Don't miss out on the world premiere of this pitch-black family drama, all about loss, legacy and learning who you are. Book your tickets for Relics at the Lyric Hammersmith today!

shows.name: Harry Potter and the Cursed Child
shows.description_short: J. K. Rowling's Wizarding World is transformed on stage, offering fans a unique return to their favourite fantasy world. Best for age 10+.
shows.typical_age_range: Best for age 10+. Please be advised that children under the age of 16 may be refused entry if they are not sitting next to an accompanying adult.
shows.work_performed_name: Harry Potter and the Cursed Child
shows.description_full:
Reuniting lifelong fans with Harry, Ron and Hermione, Harry Potter and the Cursed Child at the Palace Theatre in Soho is the spectacular continuation of J.K. Rowling’s best-selling series.

Jack Thorne’s bewitching play is set 19 years after Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows, when Harry’s son Albus has just started Hogwarts and a new threat emerges that seems very familiar. Director John Tiffany and illusionist Jamie Harrison have created a dazzling array of special effects for the Harry Potter and the Cursed Child London production, and it scooped a record-breaking nine Olivier Awards after premiering in 2016.

Set 19 years after the final book, the play follows Harry, Ron and Hermione’s children as they begin studying at Hogwarts. Harry’s middle child, Albus, is struggling with the attention that comes with being related to his famous father, whilst Harry is struggling with the pressure of a demanding job at the Ministry of Magic. The past is never far away, and when a familiar type of evil rears its head, father and son both discover that darkness can come from unexpected places.

Audiences are encouraged to #KeepTheSecret, so what happens next cannot be shared, but you can expect to see lots of familiar faces from the books and some surprising new characters as well.

With incredible stagecraft, you will feel as if you have stepped right into Hogwarts and other locations in the books, and every twist and turn is a treat for fans who want to learn more about the story’s past and future.

The play is written by Jack Thorne, based on an original story by Thorne, J. K. Rowling and the show’s director, John Tiffany. Jack Thorne is an acclaimed playwright who is also known for the Old Vic’s A Christmas Carol and the television adaptation of His Dark Materials starring James McAvoy and Ruth Wilson.

shows.name: Operation Mincemeat
shows.description_short: The zany true story of the corpse that fooled Hitler is turned into a high-spirited musical that riffs on WWII films and classic showtunes.
shows.description_full:
Did you know that World War Two was quite possibly won by a well-dressed corpse? Join a team of secret agents at Covent Garden's Fortune Theatre for the high-spirited, delightfully eccentric new musical Operation Mincemeat . Boasting 67 five-star reviews and an Olivier Award for Best New Musical, this is one of the most fun nights out in the West End, with a hardworking cast bringing slapstick humour, catchy songs and uplifting stories of friendship and love to the stage.

The year is 1943 and the Allies are losing the war. From the great young minds at MI5 comes a bizarre but brilliant idea to wrongfoot the Nazis: dress a corpse up, handcuff a briefcase of “Top Secret” documents to his arm, and wash him up on the coast of Spain.

Stranger than fiction but 100% true, this madcap plan to fool Hitler is adapted into a new musical by fringe theatre company SplitLip. Playfully dubbed “ Singin’ in the Rain meets Strangers on a Train ”, the Operation Mincemeat musical features a fast-paced plot, wicked humour and original songs including “God, That’s Brilliant”.

Operation Mincemeat is the proud owner of 67 five-star reviews - and counting! The Stage declared it a “brilliant spy thriller, a brilliant comedy, and a brilliant musical all rolled into one”, and newspapers across the UK followed suit.

This “irrepressible wartime musical is a West End triumph” (The Guardian), “a delight from start to finish” (Time Out) and a “glorious combination of ingenious, silly and surprisingly moving” (The Telegraph).

If you’re still not convinced, read theatregoer reviews below, and book your tickets for a night of high-spirited and irrepressible entertainment.

The Operation Mincemeat musical cast consists of five actors who gamely play every role. It has already tickled theatregoers’ funny bones, with sold-out runs at New Diorama Theatre, Southwark Playhouse and Riverside Studios and has extended several times since opening at the Fortune Theatre in 2023.

SplitLip’s team includes David Cumming, Felix Hagan, Natasha Hodgson and Zoë Roberts - and all four co-wrote the book, music and lyrics to this irreverent hit.

Part spoof, part plucky fringe show and complete bags of fun: you’ll need a very stiff upper lip to resist Operation Mincemeat tickets in the West End! It’s the musical everyone has been talking about, showcases the best of British talent, and will have you on your feet for a standing ovation in no time.

faq.question: Are latecomers allowed at Operation Mincemeat?
faq.answer: Wherever possible, latecomers will be admitted at a suitable break in the performance, though this may not be until the interval. If it is disruptive to seat you in your original seat during the performance, you may be asked to sit in alternative seats until the interval, when you are then able to move to your original seats.

shows.name: Paranormal Activity
shows.description_short: A couple are haunted by their past in this terrifying new play set in the spine-chilling universe of the Paranormal Activity film franchise.
shows.description_full:
Are you brave enough? Paranormal Activity , a new story set in the world of the terrifying supernatural horror film series of the same name, comes to the Ambassadors Theatre for a strictly limited run this winter. This “terrifyingly good” (The Reviews Hub) play about an American couple who relocate to London, only to find their haunted past has followed them overseas, will have you on the edge of your seat. Only the fearless need book - secure your tickets today, if you dare.

When American couple Jamie and Lou are offered an opportunity to move to London for work, they jump at the chance to relocate from their native Chicago. Hoping to escape their troubled history, the couple are eager to turn over a new leaf, to shake off the demons of their past and have a fresh start. But when sinister occurrences begin to happen in their new London home, the couple soon learn that houses aren’t haunted - people are, and the past might not be as easy to leave behind as they had hoped…

A brand new original story, set in the spine-tingling world of the Paranormal Activity film franchise, the production premiered in 2024 at the Leeds Playhouse and will have a short tour of several US cities before coming to London’s West End for a strictly limited run. Combining innovative stagecraft with psychological horror, Paranormal Activity is sure to haunt you long after you’ve left the theatre - book your tickets today.

The Leeds Playhouse premiere of the show featured Patrick Hesuinger ( Gossip Girl, Black Swan ) as James and Melissa James ( Wheel of Time, Moulin Rouge! The Musical ) as Lou, who both return to their roles in the West End.

Joining them are Jackie Morrison as Etheline Cotgrave and Pippa Winslow as Carolanne.

Paranormal Activity is written by Levi Holloway and directed by Felix Barrett.

Levi Holloway is an American Playwright and actor. He is the founder of Neverbird Project, a theatre company for deaf and hard-of-hearing youths. Holloway’s credits include Grey House on Broadway, Turret and The Haven Place .

Felix Barret is an award-winning British director and the artistic director and founder of immersive theatre company Punchdrunk. Barrett’s credits include The Drowned Man: A Hollywood Fable, The Burnt City, Sleep No More in London and on Broadway, The Duchess of Malfi with English National Opera and The Third Day for HBO and Sky Atlantic. In 2016 he was awarded an MBE for his services to theatre.

Set and costume design is by Tony nominee Fly Davis ( Caroline or Change, The Ocean at the End of the Lane ) with illusion design by magician and illusionist Chris Fisher ( Harry Potter and the Cursed Child, Back to the Future the Musical ), lighting design by Anna Watson ( Giant, A Christmas Carol ), video design by Luke Halls ( The Book of Dust, The Lehman Trilogy ) and sound design by Gareth Fry ( Til The Stars Come Down, Harry Potter and the Cursed Child ).

The production is presented by Melting Pot, the minds behind the award-winning stage adaptation of The Life of Pi and terrifying shows including Ghost Stories and The Girl on the Train , in association with Leeds Playhouse, by arrangement with Paramount Pictures.

Paranormal Activity is “unnerving entertainment sure to delight” (Wharfdale Observer). Don’t miss this edge-of-your-seat thriller as it comes to the West End for a strictly limited run. Secure your tickets for Paranormal Activity at the Ambassadors Theatre today!

shows.name: Peter Grimes
shows.description_short: Deborah Warner’s haunting production of Britten’s masterwork opera about community, guilt and the sea returns to the Royal Opera House.
shows.description_full:
Experience the brooding power of Benjamin Britten’s Peter Grimes at the Royal Opera House, in the heart of Covent Garden. Deborah Warner’s stark contemporary staging of Britten’s gripping tale of isolation, judgement and haunting tragedy returns this spring, conducted by Jakub Hrůša, Music Director of The Royal Opera. Step into the tense world of a small coastal village where suspicion and hostility grow around fisherman Peter Grimes as he battles tragedy, community suspicion and his own fragile mind - secure your tickets today.

Set in a bleak English coastal village, the story follows Peter Grimes, a fisherman whose life becomes defined by the mysterious death of his apprentice. Cleared by the inquest, Grimes faces continuing suspicion and marginalisation from the tight‑knit community. Determined to make a living, he takes on another apprentice, but tragedy strikes again, pushing him to the brink of sanity as the villagers’ mistrust grows.

Themes of alienation, moral ambiguity and the oppressive force of communal judgment are set against compelling orchestral writing, including Britten’s iconic "Four Sea Interludes", that evokes the merciless sea and the psychological landscape of the protagonist.

Adapted by Benjamin Britten and Montagu Slater from the narrative poem The Borough by George Crabbe, Peter Grimes has stood as one of the most important British operas of the 20th century. Credited with reviving English opera on the international stage, its complex protagonist and moral ambiguity have challenged audiences since its premiere at Sadler's Wells in 1945 - which also marked the reopening of the theatre following World War II. The opera’s powerful marine‑inspired orchestration has made works like the "Four Sea Interludes" an enduring part of classical concert repertoire.

The Royal Opera House has presented several key productions of Peter Grimes over the decades, and Deborah Warner’s visceral contemporary staging, which originally premiered in 2022 as part of The Royal Opera’s Britten Cycle, returns in 2026 to probe the psychological and social forces at the heart of Britten’s drama.

Casting varies by performance date. Principal cast includes:

Peter Grimes is composed by Benjamin Britten ( The Turn of the Screw, Billy Budd ) with a libretto by Montagu Slater ( The Seven Brides for Seven Brothers, Stay Down Miner ).

This Royal Opera production is directed by Deborah Warner ( Billy Budd, Wozzeck ), with set design by Michael Levine ( A Midsummer Night’s Dream, The Flying Dutchman ), costume design by Luis F. Carvalho ( Il Trovatore, Don Giovanni ), and lighting by Peter Mumford ( Die Frau ohne Schatten, The Minotaur ). Movement direction is by Kim Brandstrup ( The Death of Klinghoffer, Les Enfants Terribles ). Music Director of The Royal Opera, Jakub Hrůša ( Rusalka, Lohengrin ), conducts.

Britten’s score pulses with emotional depth, from introspective melodies to storm‑laden orchestral surges. Whether you’re discovering Britten’s evocative world for the first time or returning to a masterwork, Deborah Warner’s acclaimed staging of this haunting melodrama promises an unmissable operatic experience this spring. Book your tickets for Peter Grimes at the Royal Opera House today!

shows.name: The Marriage of Figaro
shows.description_short: David McVicar’s witty, twist‑filled staging of Mozart’s iconic comic opera about servants, scandal and love returns to London.
shows.description_full:
Get muddled up in one unforgettable day in Count Almaviva’s household with Mozart’s The Marriage of Figaro at the Royal Opera House, in vibrant Covent Garden, this summer. Servant’s Fiagro and Susanna are preparing to marry - but when their employer, the jealous Count intervines, a tangled web of jealousy, disguise and mischief threatens to upend their plans. Giedrė Šlekytė conducts Sir David McVicar’s acclaimed revival, which transplants the action to 1830, on the eve of Revolution - balancing class commentary, laugh‑out‑loud comedy and touching moments of forgiveness and reconciliation. Secure your tickets today!

Set over the course of a single “mad day,” clever plans, disguises and intrigues unfold as Figaro, valet to Count Almaviva, and his fiancée Susanna prepare for their wedding - only to be thwarted by the Count’s inappropriate attention toward Susanna.

Figaro and Susanna team up with the long-suffering Countess to outwit the Count, while Cherubino - the Count’s page - pursues his youthful crushes, and long‑buried truths are revealed in a finale filled with forgiveness and celebration. Mozart’s sparkling score is rich with character, wit and empathy, and has made Figaro , and songs such as “Dove sono” and “Non più andrai”, a cornerstone of the operatic repertory for over two centuries.

Le nozze di Figaro , or The Marriage of Figaro, is based on Pierre Beaumarchais’ play Le Mariage de Figaro, a sequel to The Barber of Seville , famously adapted by Gioachino Rossini, and marked the first collaboration between Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart and librettist Lorenzo Da Ponte - the pair would go on to collaborate further on Don Giovanni and Così fan tutte . The opera premiered in 1786 at the Burgtheater in Vienna on 1 May 1786, with Mozart himself conducting the first two performances,

Beaumarchais’ original play was scandalous in its day, banned for its bold satire of aristocratic privilege and commentary on class inequality. To bring it to the Viennese stage, Da Ponte softened much of the political edge while preserving its biting wit and intricate plot. The resulting opera was a triumph: The Marriage of Figaro quickly sold out in Vienna and became a sensation in Prague, where Mozart wrote of hearing “nothing but Figaro!” in the streets.

The Royal Opera’s staging by David McVicar, first seen in 2006, moves the action to a French château in 1830, on the eve of the July Revolution. McVicar’s interpretation delves into the play’s class tensions and revolutionary undercurrents while celebrating the opera’s humour, energy and emotional nuance - qualities that have made Figaro a timeless favourite for over two centuries.

The cast of this production varies depending on the performance date. Principal casting includes:

The Marriage of Figaro features music by Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart with a libretto by Lorenzo Da Ponte.

This production is directed by Sir David McVicar ( Salome, The Magic Flute ), with set and costume design by Tanya McCallin ( War and Peace, Eugene Onegin ), lighting by Paule Constable ( The Turn of the Screw, Peter Grimes ), and movement direction by Leah Hausman ( The Barber of Seville, Rodelinda ). Giedrė Šlekytė ( Hansel and Gretel ) conducts, returning for her second season following a triumphant Royal Opera House debut in 2025

Join Figaro and Susanna for a wedding day full of surprises! Mozart’s The Marriage of Figaro is one of opera’s greatest comic masterpieces, celebrated for its ingenious blend of social satire, charming characters and breathtaking music. Sir David McVicar’s joyful, clever and musically sumptuous staging offers an outing that’s perfect for opera lovers and first‑timers alike, so RSVP to the wedding of the season - book tickets for The Marriage of Figaro at the Royal Opera House today!

shows.name: So Are We: León & Lightfoot
shows.description_short: Royal Ballet presents Shoot the Moon and Salle de Dance, two stirring dance shows by choreographer duo Sol León and Paul Lightfoot.
shows.description_full:
The Royal Ballet Company presents an exciting double-bill of contemporary work this year, as Sol León and Paul Lightfoot’s So Are We opens at the Royal Opera House near Covent Garden. Comprising two individual shows - Shoot the Moon and Salle de Dance - brought together for one exhilarating performance, this is a stirring, passionate mix of old and new from the acclaimed choreographer team, and marks the first time a British company has performed the duo’s work.

So Are We is a mixed programme that matches Leon & Lightfoot’s classic work Shoot the Moon with newly staged piece Salle de Dance .

Shoot the Moon celebrates its 20th anniversary this year, following its world premiere in the Netherlands in 2006. This intimate piece reflects on themes of solitude within relationships, as three couples teeter on the edge of intimacy and separation. With its expressive, fluid movement and yearning music, this is a contemporary classic that stands the test of time. It is scored by Academy Award-winning American composer Philip Glass ( The Hours, The Truman Show ) and his melodic Tirol Concerto.

Salle de Dance is a new world premiere stage performance, adapted from the filmed version by Leon & Lightfoot. The original version was created during lockdown as a celebration of the balletic art form, and it now leaps onto the stages that inspired it. Inspired by a dancer’s daily study of ballet classes, it is performed on a bare stage, with the power of dance right at the fore. It is set to a new score by award-winning composer Ilya Demutsky ( A Hero of Our Time ).

Sol León and Paul Lightfoot are a ground-breaking choreography duo who have worked extensively together on some of the best 20th and 21st century dance performances. Since 1959, they have created a huge number of works, including more than 50 for Nederlands Dans Theater, of which they were in-house choreographers between 2002 and 2020.

Best known for their expressive, abstract and often minimalist style and strong sense of collaboration, their work includes Skew Whiff, Subject to Change and Schubert .

This daring two-part show is a fantastic introduction to one of Europe’s most visionary dance creative teams, brought together in London for a unique evening of entertainment. Don’t miss out on seeing Leon & Lightfoot’s boundary-pushing work at the Royal Opera House, with tickets for So Are We available now.

shows.name: Archduke
shows.description_short: Pulitzer finalist Ravji Joseph's darkly comic play reimagines the events leading up to the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand.
shows.description_full:
Innovative new play Archduke, from Pulitzer Prize finalist Rajiv Joseph, makes its London debut this summer at the Royal Court Theatre in the heart of Sloane Square. This explosive, darkly comic fictionalised imagining of the events leading up to the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand is “an ambitious mix of history, comedy and tragedy” (LA Times). You won't want to miss it - secure your tickets today.

1914: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand sparks a series of events that, within a month, trigger the beginning of World War I.

But who were the perpetrators of this shockingly pivotal event? While Franz Ferdinand’s name looms large in the history books, you’ve likely never heard the name of the man who pulled the trigger before - Gavrilo Princip.

When 19-year-old Gavrilo receives a terminal tuberculosis diagnosis, he fears he is running out of time - he wants to start a revolution, he wants to make a difference in this world, and he doesnt want to die a virgin. Starved for purpose, and food, Gavrilo and two of his fellow consumptives are desperate to be remembered. So when Serbian nationalist Captain Dragutin “Apis” Dimitrijevic offers them a chance to leave their mark on history, the dying young men are soon swept along in a plot that will change the world forever.

A “deft play of fact and fiction” (Philadelphia Inquirer), Archduke boldly retells the motivations of the group of young men who planned the assassination, framing them not as sinister masterminds, but as teenagers hungry, hapless, and susceptible to radicalisation.

Archduke first premiered in Los Angeles in 2017 and was also staged in Philadelphia, before making an Off-Broadway debut in November 2025. This production marks the show's European premiere.

Casting for this production is to be announced

Archduke is written by Rajiv Joseph. Joseph is a two-time Obie Award winner and a Lucille Lortel Award winner. His notable works include Guards at the Taj, Shalimar the Clown , and Bengal Tiger at the Baghdad Zoo (which premiered on Broadway starring Robin Williams) for which he was a finalist for the 2010 Pulitzer Prize for drama.

Lyndsey Turner directs. Turner is an Olivier winner and an Associate Director at the Sheffield Crucible and The Gate Theatre. Her credits include Chimerica, Machinal, Fathers and Sons, and Posh .

Production design is by Tony and Olivier winner Es Devlin, whose credits include The Lehman Trilogy, Chimerica, The Motive and the Cue, and design for the London and Rio Olympic ceremonies.

This “slyly relevant new period dramedy” (Variety) is a stark reminder that history is often shaped by those least qualified. Pulitzer finalist Ravji Joseph’s innovative, daring and alarmingly prescient play about radicalisation, morality and mortality is unmissable. Book tickets for Archduke at the Royal Court Theatre today.

shows.name: I Puritani
shows.description_short: Bellini’s bel canto masterpiece returns to the ROH for the first time in 30 years, in a bold new staging by Olivier-winner Richard Jones.
shows.description_full:
Experience operatic romance and vocal fireworks as I Puritani makes a grand return to the Royal Opera House, in the heart of Covent Garden. Directed by Richard Jones, this production marks the first time the company has performed I Puritani in over thirty years - don’t miss this rare chance to see Bellini’s emotionally charged masterpiece about the tender bonds of love, the devastation of heartbreak, and the elation of reconciliation. Secure your tickets today.

Set during the English Civil War, I Puritani follows the doomed love between Elvira, the daughter of a Puritan governor, and Arturo, a Royalist supporter. On the eve of their wedding, political allegiances and suspicions intervene, throwing Elvira into a spiral of heartbreak and madness. As the tides of war shift, love and loyalty are tested to their limits in a powerful operatic exploration of obsession, faith, and forgiveness.

With its sweeping score, elaborate choruses, and dramatic flair, I Puritani is a towering example of bel canto opera. Bellini’s final composition is renowned for its vocal intricacy, and this new production transforms the narrative into a psychological portrait, heightening the tension and theatricality. Expect passion, betrayal, and vocal pyrotechnics in a production that brings gothic surrealism to Bellini’s haunting vision.

Bellini’s I Puritani stands as one of the most celebrated works of the bel canto era, balancing lyrical beauty with dramatic depth. Based on Têtes Rondes et Cavaliers , a historical play about Puritans and Cavaliers during the English Civil War, the opera was first staged in 1835 at Paris’s Théâtre‑Italien and quickly became a favourite for its radiant melodies and expressive vocal writing. The score features some of Bellini’s most beloved music, including Elvira’s virtuosic arias and the intense ensemble writing in its final act. Internationally, I Puritani continues to delight audiences with its emotional range - from tender romance to heartrending despair and ultimately to triumph.

Bellini’s final work, the opera remains a testament to his mastery of long, lyrical vocal lines and dramatic pacing, securing its place among the great masterpieces of the 19th‑century Italian repertory. Despite its initial success and stunning musical passages, it has long been underperformed compared to Bellini’s more popular works, such as Norma or La sonnambula . This new Royal Opera House production marks the company’s first staging of the opera in over three decades, and offers a rare opportunity to experience its full splendour.

I Puritani was composed by Vincenzo Bellini ( Norma, La sonnambula, Il pirata ), with a libretto by Carlo Pepoli,

The Royal Opera’s new staging of I Puritani is directed by Richard Jones ( Boris Godunov, The Mastersingers of Nuremberg ), one of the UK’s most celebrated opera directors, known for his bold, stylised interpretations. Riccardo Frizza ( La sonnambula, Norma, Don Pasquale ) conducts, making his Royal Opera debut.

Design is by Hyemi Shin ( The Cunning Little Vixen, The Rape of Lucretia, A Little Night Music ), with costumes by Nicky Gillibrand ( Billy Budd, The Magic Flute, The Light Princess ). Lighting is by Adam Silverman ( Pelléas et Mélisande, Katya Kabanova, Jenůfa ), and movement direction by Sarah Fahie ( The Marriage of Figaro, Der Rosenkavalier, Carmen ).

Casting varies by performance date. Principal cast includes:

Opera fans, rejoice! I Puritani promises a night of exceptional singing, rare repertoire, and theatrical flair. Richard Jones’s vision casts a stark, stylised light on the opera’s themes of madness and political extremism, in a haunting and expressionist staging. This is one of opera’s most exciting offerings of the year. Let Bellini’s romantic drama sweep you away - book tickets for I Puritani at the Royal Opera House today.

shows.name: La Boheme
shows.description_short: Richard Jones’ acclaimed staging of Puccini’s timeless tale of bohemian love, laughter and heartbreak returns to the Royal Opera House.
shows.description_full:
Step into the vibrant streets of 19th‑century Paris with La Boheme at the Royal Opera House, in the centre of Covent Garden. Puccini’s beloved opera captures the spirit of youth, community and passion, following a group of young artists - a poet, painter, musician and philosopher - as they navigate love, poverty and friendship against the backdrop of the Latin Quarter at Christmastime. Secure your tickets today.

La Boheme tells the story of a group of young bohemians living in the Latin Quarter of Paris in the mid‑19th century, chronicling their dreams, struggles and enduring friendships. At its heart is the poet Rodolfo, whose life changes when he meets the delicate seamstress Mimì on a cold Christmas Eve. Their instant connection sparks a tender romance, but poverty and illness soon threaten their happiness.

As well as scenes of laughter and warmth, from shared meals in their garret to playful flirtations at Café Momus, there are also moments of profound sorrow as reality encroaches on the lovers’ hopes. Puccini’s luminous score, featuring some of opera’s most cherished arias such as “Sì, mi chiamano Mimì” and Musetta’s waltz “Quando m’en vo’,” brings emotional depth to every relationship on stage.

Puccini’s La Boheme premiered at Turin’s Teatro Regio in 1896 and quickly became one of the most performed operas worldwide. Its intimate portrayal of love, youth and loss - drawn from Henri Murger’s Scènes de la vie de bohème - has made it a staple of the operatic repertoire. La Boheme has been a beloved favourite at the Royal Opera House for generations, and the most performed opera at the venue. Jones’ acclaimed staging was first performed for the Royal Opera House in 2017, and at the time of its premiere was the first new production of La Boheme for the theatre in four decades.

The opera’s blend of humour, lyricism and heartbreak has inspired countless productions and recordings, and its principal arias remain central to the lyric tenor and soprano repertories. The show has also inspired several adaptations, most famously Jonathan Larson’s hit musical Rent , which transplants the action to New York in the early 90s.

The cast of this production varies depending on the performance date. Principal cast includes:

La Boheme is composed by Giacomo Puccini ( Tosca, Madama Butterfly ) with a libretto by Giuseppe Giacosa and Luigi Illica ( Tosca ). The production is directed by Richard Jones (Handel’s Giulio Cesare, Peter Grimes ), with design by Stewart Laing ( Dead Man Walking ), lighting by Mimi Jordan Sherin ( Boris Godunov ), and movement direction by Sarah Fahie ( Der Rosenkavalier ). Conducting duties are shared by Christopher Willis ( Le nozze di Figaro ) and Lorenzo Passerini ( Faust ), making his Opera House debut.

Become a Bohemian this summer and join in the joys and sorrows that unfold through soaring duets, intimate arias and evocative choral scenes. Whether you’re a Rent fan exploring opera for the first time or an opera aficionado returning to a favourite, Richard Jones’ acclaimed staging offers a richly emotional experience with world‑class singing, evocative staging and Puccini’s unforgettable score. Book your tickets for La Boheme at the Royal Opera House today!

shows.name: La Fille du regiment
shows.description_short: A tomboy raised by soldiers seeks love and identity amid military life and surprise family secrets in Donizetti’s Bel canto comic opera.
shows.description_full:
Atteeention! March on down to the Royal Opera House, in the heart of Covent Garden, for La fille du regiment this summer. Gaetano Donizetti’s Bel canto comic opera, about a precocious girl raised by a troop of soldiers, is packed with vivacity and vocal fireworks and comes to life in Laurent Pelly’s humorous new staging, starring Juan Diego Flórez, Jonah Hoskins, and Sara Blanch, making her Royal Opera House debut. You won't want to miss it - secure your tickets today.

Set during the Napoleonic Wars in the Tyrol, the opera tells the story of Marie, a vivandière (aka canteen girl) adopted and raised by the 21st Regiment of French soldiers after being found as an infant on the battlefield. When Tonio, a young Tyrolean peasant, declares his love for Marie and joins the regiment to be with her, the path to their happiness seems clear. However, when the aristocratic Marquise de Berkenfield appears and lays claim to Marie, a long‑buried secret about her origins comes to light, threatening their plans and leading to lively comedy, heartfelt moments and, ultimately, a celebration of love and community.

La fille du regiment (which translates as The Daughter of the Regiment ) is an opéra comique in two acts by Gaetano Donizetti to a French libretto by Jules‑Henri Vernoy de Saint‑Georges and Jean‑François Bayard. Donizetti composed La fille du regiment while living in Paris in the late 1830s, and it was his first opera written directly in French. Since its premiere at the Opéra‑Comique on 11 February 1840, the work has become a beloved staple of the operatic repertoire, admired for its blend of lively ensemble numbers, bright orchestration and comic dialogue.

One of the opera’s most famous moments is Tonio’s aria “Ah! mes amis, quel jour de fête!”, renowned for its taxing series of eight (often nine) high Cs, a highlight for tenors and a crowd‑pleasing showstopper. Laurent Pelly’s engaging staging - a co‑production with the Vienna State Opera and the Metropolitan Opera, New York - evokes the military camp and aristocratic tensions with imaginative design and humour, making this production a lively experience for both seasoned opera lovers and newcomers alike.

Casting varies by performance date. Principal cast includes:

The spoken role of the Duchesse de Crakentorp is often a surprise highlight of La fille du régiment . In past Royal Opera House revivals, the part has been played by stars including Dawn French, Ann Widdecombe, Miranda Richardson, and Kiri Te Kanawa. Casting for the role in this production is award-winning actor Tamsein Greig.

La fille du regiment is directed by Laurent Pelly ( Lakmé, Don Pasquale, Cendrillon ), who also designed the costumes for the show. Giuseppe Finzi ( La bohème, L’elisir d’amore, Rigoletto ) conducts.

Dialogue is prepared by Agathe Mélinand (L’Impresario de Smyrne, Gypsy ), set design is by Chantal Thomas ( La Bohème, Manon ), lighting by Joël Adam ( Wozzeck, The Magic Flute ), and choreography is by Laura Scozzi ( Orpheus in the Underworld, La Dolce Vita ).

The music is by Gaetano Donizetti ( L’elisir d’amore, Lucia di Lammermoor, Don Pasquale ), with a French libretto by Jules‑Henri Vernoy de Saint‑Georges and Jean‑François Bayard.

Ready for high spirits and even higher Cs? La fille du regiment is a feel‑good operatic comedy full of charm, catchy melodies and thrilling vocal feats. From its touching heroine to its exuberant choruses and spirited duets, this production is an ideal night out for dedicated opera fans and newbies alike. Book your tickets for La fille du regiment at the Royal Opera House now and enjoy a summer of laughs, love and bel canto brilliance

shows.description_full:
Embark on a Roarsome adventure this summer as Dinosaur World Live returns to London due to popular demand. Coming to the Wembley Park Theatre, in the heart of Wembley, this Olivier-winning, family-friendly show features jaw-dropping puppets and audience interaction in a vivid prehistoric world. Little ones can even meet the dinos afterwards - if they’re brave enough! Head off on a Jurassic adventure - book today!

In a prehistoric world, the terrifying Tyrannosaurus Rex lurks in wait for its prey - but T-Rex's arent the only creatures you will meet as Triceratops, Giraffatitans, Microraptors and Segnosaurus all roam the stage in this epic and educational show. Join a gang of intrepid explorers as they head on a thrilling journey back to Jurassic times, featuring astonishingly life-like puppets and audience interaction.

This show is dino-mite! Travel back in time on a thrilling trail through the Jurassic forests, and meet all your favourite dinosaurs in this award-winning interactive show. shows.name: Malory Towers
shows.description_short: The triumphant musical, adapted and directed by Emma Rice, heads to the Alexandra Palace for a limited summer season. Best for age 8+.
shows.typical_age_range: Best for age 8+. Children under 16 must be accompanied by and sat next to an adult.
shows.description_full:
Embark on a journey to Alexandra Palace in London, where the magnificent Grade II listed building between Wood Green and Muswell Hill will transform into the sun-soaked cliffs of Cornwall for a glorious musical adaptation of Malory Towers . Book your tickets for your dose of nostalgia and inspire the next generation of Enid Blyton lovers along the way this summer.

As one of the most beloved children’s book series, Malory Towers charts the journey of Darrell Rivers as she leaves home and enrolls at a girls’ boarding school. Largely centred on the friendship between Darrell and Gwendoline, Malory Towers is a wonderful story about personal growth, loyalty, and community.

Malory Towers was published as a book series between 1946 and 1951, with six more additions added by Pamela Cox in 2009. A TV series based on the series was first broadcast on CBBC in 2020, running for seven years until its final episode aired in October 2025.

The stage musical was first presented at The Passenger Shed in Bristol, where it debuted in 2019, before performing in Cambridge, York, Exeter, Manchester and Oxford. This production at the Alexandra Palace is part of the latest UK tour, which was postponed in 2020.

Malory Towers has been adapted and directed by Emma Rice. This musical is co-produced with David Pugh and Dafydd Rogers.

Emma is known for her fearless work and was the Artistic Director of Shakespeare’s Globe from 2016 to 2018 before launching her touring theatre company, Wise Children. Her infamous adaptations include Brief Encounter, The Red Shoes and Pandora’s Box .

The rest of the creative team includes set and costume by Lez Brotherston, music by Ian Ross, sound and video by Simon Baker, lighting by Malcolm Rippeth and movement and choreography by Alistair David.

Malory Towers is a musical for girls, boys and all grown-up children who still dream of midnight feasts and want to capture the series of raucous stories once more on the stage. Book your tickets to see this sparky and brilliant stage adaptation at the Alexandra Palace for a limited time this summer.

shows.name: Fences
shows.description_short: Daniel Bailey (Red Pitch) directs this major revival of August Wilson’s landmark play about racial tensions in 1950s Pittsburgh.
shows.typical_age_range: Best for age 14+. Children under 16 must be accompanied by and sat next to an adult.
shows.description_full:
August Wilson’s landmark play, Fences , comes to the Lyric Hammersmith, in the heart of Hammersmith, this October in a huge new revival from Red Pitch director Daniel Bailey. This Pulitzer and Tony-winning drama follows Troy, the patriarch of a working-class Black family, as he grapples with his past in the changing racial landscape of the 1950s. This “powerfully captivating” (London Theatre) play comes to London for a strictly limited run this autumn - don’t miss it.

Formerly the star player in the Black American baseball league, Troy Maxson now works a blue-collar job to try to provide for his wife and child. Having cheated death once before, Troy will do whatever it takes to survive. While Troy is haunted by his past, his wife Rose holds the family together, facing the changing landscape of 1950’s Pittsburgh with an unrelenting hope that the future will be brighter for their teenage son, Cory. But as Cory’s dreams clash with Troy’s fears, tempers flare, sparking a reckoning that will change the Maxsons’ lives forever.

Fences premiered at the Yale Repertory Theatre in 1985, receiving its Broadway debut in 1987. A critical success, the production, which starred James Earl Jones as Troy, won several Tony awards and the Pulitzer Prize for drama. A later revival starring Denzel Washington and Viola Davis also won several Tony awards, including for Best Revival, with Davis and Washington later reprising their roles in the 2016 Academy Award-nominated film adaptation. The show’s UK premiere was in 1990, at the Liverpool Playhouse. The play's most notable UK revival was a 2013 staging at Theatre Royal Bath, starring Lenny Henry, which later transferred to the West End.

Casting is to be announced.

Fences is written by August Wilson (1945 – 2005). Often considered to be one of the most important Black American writers, Wilson is best known for his “Pittsburgh Cycle”, which includes Fences, ten plays set in the city, chronicling the African-American experience throughout the 20th Century. His works include Ma Rainey's Black Bottom, Joe Turner's Come and Gone, and The Piano Lesson . Wilson was an inductee of the American Theatre Hall of Fame, a Pulitzer and Tony winner and an Academy Award and Emmy nominee.

Daniel Bailey directs. Bailey is a dramaturg, director, and writer, and a former Associate Director at the Bush Theatre. His credits include Red Pitch at the Bush, in the West End and in Washington DC, August in England with Lenny Henry at The Bush, The American Dream 2.0 at the Young Vic and Dropped for Channel 4.

This scintillating drama from one of America’s most influential writers of the 20th Century comes to London for a strictly limited run this October. Exploring ambition, racial politics and the legacy of the past, you won’t want to miss this “bruisingly powerful” (Independent) Tony and Pulitzer-winning play. Secure your tickets for Fences at the Lyric Hammersmith today.

shows.name: Angel's Bone
shows.description_short: Visionary theatre director Kip Williams makes his UK opera debut with a haunting new production of English National Opera's Angel's Bone.
shows.description_full:
This Autumn, Angel’s Bone , one of the most significant operatic works of the past decade, plays at the London Coliseum, just off Leicester Square, in a momentous revival from the English National Opera. Directed by Kip Williams, this dark fable delves into the harrowing realities of modern-day slavery and human trafficking. Secure your tickets to English National Opera: Angel’s Bone today.

A desperate couple who long for a better life, Angel’s Bone follows what happens when Mr and Mrs X.E. discover two wounded angels in their garden. As they set out to nurse them back to health, what begins as an act of apparent compassion quickly spirals into a stark look at greed and exploitation. Set to a pulsating score that fuses medieval polyphony, punk, electronics, chamber music and cabaret, Angel’s Bone will grip audiences with its unflinching take on human trafficking and modern-day slavery.

Composed by Du Yun and with a libretto by the playwright and filmmaker, Royce Vavrek, the “disturbing, powerful and original” (The Washington Post) Angel’s Bone made its world premiere in 2016 in New York. It was staged as part of the Prototype Festival and received the 2017 Pulitzer Prize for Music.

It was staged again in Asia at the Kwai Tsing Theatre in Hong Kong in 2018, before a streamed production by The Ventura College was streamed in 2021.

This new production by Kip Williams will receive a UK premiere in Greater Manchester in May, before heading to the London Coliseum from October 2026. The Manchester production is in collaboration with the BBC Philharmonic Orchestra and Kantos Chamber Choir.

Allison Cook, a Scottish mezzo-soprano and singing actress, will star as Mrs X.E. in this new production of Angel’s Bone . Her other credits with the English National Opera include the 2024 production of Duke Bluebeard’s Castle . Rodney Earl Clake, who has opera and musical theatre experience, will star as Mr X.E.

Mariam Wallentin will star in the role of Girl Angel. The Swedish cross-genre musician and voice actress will make her ENO debut in this production, but is best known as one half of the acclaimed duo Wildbirds & Peacedrums. She will be joined by the Scottish tenor Matthew McKinney in the role of Boy Angel.

Angel’s Bone is composed by Du Yun, the first Asian woman to receive the Pulitzer Prize for Music. Her other opera credits include In Our Daughter’s Eyes and Sweet Land .

The multi-award-winning theatre and opera director Kip Williams will make his UK opera debut with this production of Angel’s Bone . His recent credits include Dracula starring Cynthia Erivo, The Maids at Donmar Warehouse and The Picture of Dorian Gray starring Sarah Snook.

Marg Horwell, the Olivier Award-winning designer, who has collaborated with Kip, is also working on this production. Some of her other credits include The Seagull and Opera Australia’s production of Carmen . Completing the creative team is Jack Knowles as Lighting Designer, Ash J Woodward as Video Designer, and Dominic Bilkey and Joseph Reiser, who will be the Co-Sound Designers. The internationally renowned conductor Baldur Brönniman will lead the performances.

You won’t want to miss your chance to experience Du Yun’s “audacious and searing” (The New York Times) opera as it takes to the stage for the first time in nearly a decade. Prepare to be completely enthralled by Kip Williams genius artistry – secure your tickets for English National Opera: Angel’s Bone at the London Coliseum today.

shows.name: Tosca
shows.description_short: Explore the art of resistance and love in the face of political censorship in Annilese Miskimmon's reimagining of the timeless opera.
shows.typical_age_range: Best for age 15+. Under 5s not permitted.
shows.description_full:
Head to the London Coliseum, just off Leicester Square, to witness Annilese Miskimmon’s gripping new production of Tosca . Playing for a limited time, Puccini’s timeless opera intertwines one woman’s fight for freedom with the political landscape of 19th-century Rome. Secure your tickets to English National Opera: Tosca today.

Tosca is based on Victorien Sardou’s 1887 play, La Tosca . The story follows beautiful singer Tosca, who is deeply in love with artist Cavaradossi, but corrupt and cruel police chief Scarpia is competing for her affections. When Scarpia arrests Cavaradossi for aiding an escaped political prisoner, he gives Tosca a choice: reveal the prisoner’s location or watch Cavaradossi die. As Tosca becomes more desperate to reject Scarpia’s advances and derail his plans, she resorts to deadly tactics.

Composed by Giacomo Puccini with a libretto by Luigi Illica and Giuseppe Giacosa, Tosca first premiered in Rome in 1900, where many Italian political and cultural figures were in attendance. It has had mammoth success across the world since, and it has been featured in the English National Opera’s programme a lot over the years. Some of the notable productions of Tosca include Catherine Malfitano’s productions in 2010 and 2016. The last revival by Christof Loy was in 2022, starring Sinéad Campbell-Wallace and Adam Smith.

Axelle Fanyo makes her ENO debut in the role of Tosca. She is a Grammy Award-winning singer for the Best Opera Recording of Adriana Mater , where she starred in the role of Refka. Other credits include Les Contes d’Hoffmann , Poulenc’s Dialogues des Carmélites and Verdi’s Luisa Miller.

Tosca is conducted by the internationally-renowned Ben Glassberg.

Previously Artistic Director of the English National Opera, Annilese Miskimmon directs this production of Tosca at the London Coliseum. She has worked in some of the largest international opera houses around the world and was previously the Artistic Director and General Manager of the Danish National Opera. Her other credits include the musical productions of Bernstein’s On the Town and the world premiere of Pink Floyd’s French Revolution-based musical Ca Ira .

Amanda Holden translated this production of Tosca .

If you want to experience “opera at its most passionate and stinging” (Alexander Russell) later this year, then Tosca is just the show for you. See one of the most timeless stories unravel in a world of desire, fear, and resistance, not to be missed. Secure your tickets for English National Opera: Tosca at the London Coliseum today.

shows.name: Tango After Dark
shows.description_short: Experience the intoxicating heat of the Argentine tango with Tango After Dark, back in London after its sold-out run in 2018.
shows.typical_age_range: Ages 5+. Under 4s not permitted. Children under 16 must be accompanied by and sat next to an adult.
shows.description_full:
Escape to the city of Buenos Aires for the ultimate evening of romance and drama with Tango After Dark . Inspired by Argentina’s rich cultural tradition, German Cornejo presents a “Splendid, sensual, imposing extravagance of Argentine tango at its very best” (Dance Tabs) performed by world-class dancers from across the world. Travel to the Sadler's Wells Theatre, a short walk from the heart of Islington, for a “mesmerising spectacle” (The Mail on Sunday), which plays for a strictly limited run.

The theatrical experience draws inspiration from Astor Piazzolla, a popular Argentine tango composer who revolutionised the dance in the 1960s and 70s by incorporating elements of jazz and classical music. Tango After Dark takes this strong influence and immerses audiences into the heart of Buenos Aires. It is performed by ten of the world’s finest dancers alongside a live orchestra of five musicians and two singers.

Tango After Dark made its world premiere in London at the Peacock Theatre back in 2018, where it sold out its run. It returned to the same venue a few years later in 2022. This electric dance show has gone on to play across the globe and been praised by well-known celebrities like Simon Cowell, Jennifer Lopez and Heidi Klum. Recently, the show has been playing to packed out venues in Canada and the US on a tour this year.

The cast of Tango After Dark is led by World Tango Champions German Cornejo and Gisela Galeassi. An astonishingly athletic and powerful performer, Cornejo has also choreographed many other shows, including Immortal Tango and Tango Fire . They are joined by a group of 8 world-class tango dancers.

If you want to experience authentic Argentine tango thrust into the modern world, book Tango After Dark tickets for a “scorching display of smouldering tango, impressive acrobatics and thrilling set pieces” (Dancing Times). Let the power of dance warm up the colder evenings for you this November.

shows.name: Iphigenie en Tauride
shows.description_short: This rarely performed Gluck masterwork, based on a Greek tragedy, premieres at the ENO in a new staging by Olivier winner Lindsay Turner.
shows.description_full:
Iphigenie en Tauride , the rarely performed masterwork opera by Christoph Gluck, makes its English National Opera debut at the London Coliseum, in the heart of Leicester Square. Performed in the original French, this brand new staging is directed by Olivier Award-winner Lindsey Turner. Based on the play by Euripides, this stirring opera tells the story of two siblings who must fight their fate to reunite. Book your tickets today.

Iphigene and her brother Orestes are part of an ancient family, the inheritors of a bloody legacy that dates back to the dawn of time itself. The siblings are separated: Iphegene stranded on a remote island at the world's end, at the beck and call of a terrifying ruler, and Orestes is brought to the brink of madness. Both fear the other is dead, but dream of someday returning home.

When an enigmatic stranger washes up on the shores of Iphegenie's island, bringing news of the collapse of her family, the hope that she will someday see her brother again is shattered, and she believes the worst must have happened. But there may be more to the stranger than it first seems, and the situation is perhaps not quite as hopeless as Iphigenie fears…

Based on the Ancient Greek play Iphigenia in Tauris by Euripides, the Opera premiered in 1779 at the Paris Opera to critical acclaim. Gluck later created a German version in 1781 to celebrate the visit of the Russian Grand Duke Paul to Vienna. This remains Gluck's only opera in German. In 1889, a new German arrangement was made by Richard Strauss, which is more frequently performed than Gluck’s original. Widely regarded as Gluck’s most important work, this new staging of Gluck’s rarely performed gem, in the original French, marks the production's ENO debut.

Iphigenie en Tauride is composed by Christoph Gluck ( Orfeo ed Euridice, Alceste ), with an original French libretto by Nicolas-François Guillard ( Arvire et Evelina, La Mort d'Adam ). Baroque specialist David Bates ( Die Zauberflote, Dido and Aeneas ) conducts, making his ENO debut. Olivier winner Lyndsey Turner ( The Crucible, Chimerica ) directs.

Don’t miss this rarely staged masterpiece, as Gluck’s most powerful and important opera, makes its ENO debut in a brand new staging, with an award-winning creative team. This production has a strictly limited run - so secure your tickets for Iphigenie en Tauride at the London Coliseum today.

shows.name: Adriana Mater
shows.description_short: This new translation of Kaija Saariaho’s powerful, rarely staged opera follows a young woman raising a son during a brutal civil war.
shows.description_full:
Blistering opera Adriana Mater comes to London this winter in a brand new, specially commissioned translation for the English National Opera. This powerful, rarely staged opera about a young woman raising a son in the brutality of war is by acclaimed Finnish composer Kaija Saariaho and comes to the London Coliseum, in the heart of Leicester Square, for a strictly limited season.

During a brutal civil war in an unnamed country, a young woman, Adriana, falls pregnant after being violently assaulted by a soldier, Tsargo. Against the advice of her sister, Refka, Adriana chooses to keep the child, whom she names Yonas, concealing the truth of his origins from her son. Raised to believe in love and hope, the optimistic young man later learns the real story of how he was conceived, and is compelled to seek revenge. Can Adriana convince Yonas that true strength lies in mercy, or will he be consumed by the inherited cycle of male violence?

Picking up where the renowned opera Tosca leaves off, Adriana Mater contributes to a bold narrative season at the ENO, centring stories of resistance, resilience, freedom and hope. The opera can be watched as a stand-alone story or paired as a double bill with Tosca .

Adriana Mater premiered at the Opéra Bastille in Paris in 2006, directed by Peter Sellars. The opera later debuted in Finland in 2008, and also received premieres in the UK in a concert performance and in the US later that same year.

Adriana Mater is written by award-winning, long-term collaborators, late Finnish composer Kaija Saariaho and French writer Amin Maalouf. Music is by Saariaho ( Innocence, L'Amour de loin ), with a libretto by Maalouf ( La Passion de Simone, Emilie ). The English translation is by Saariaho’s son, Grammy winner Aleksi Barriere ( True Fire, Innocence ).

ENO Music Director Designate Andre de Ridder ( Only the Sound Remains, Drei Frauen ) conducts, with direction by Annilese Miskimmon ( Madama Butterfly, I Puritani ), with the production marking the pair's first creative collaboration.

Don’t miss this rarely staged gem of an opera by one of the most influential voices in contemporary classical music, starring ENO favourites and acclaimed singers making their Coliseum debut. Book for Adriana Mater at the London Coliseum today.
