shows.work_performed_name: There's a Bear on My Chair
shows.description_full:
Have you heard about a mischievous pair… the mouse and the bear? They’re heading to London for a live show! Ross Collins’ children’s picture book There’s a Bear on My Chair comes to the Southbank Centre’s Queen Elizabeth Hall, a short walk from Waterloo Station, for a special festive season. If your kids have made you read this brilliant story over and over again at bedtime, why not take a break, get comfy and get tickets to the “superb” (Everything Theatre) stage adaptation this Christmas.

Poor Mouse isn’t having a good day - a Bear has sat down in his favourite chair, and there’s no space left to share! As he tries all sorts of tricks to get Bear to move, Mouse suddenly has an idea… he’ll go to Bear’s house and refuse to leave.

Full of cheeky humour, engaging characters, and amazing puppetry, There’s a Bear on My Chair Live is an enchanting show for little ones and their parents.

This super-fun family adventure is based on illustrator and author Ross Collins’ picture book, which makes it great for children who are visiting the theatre for the very first time. It's a familiar story and simple rhyming plot that will keep them in their seats the whole way through.

Collins’ other books include The Elephantom and This Zoo Is Not For You .

This “treat for little ones” (LondonTheatre1) is adapted and directed by Toby Olie, whose passion for puppetry and performance began when he was just six years old. His work includes performing as Joey in War Horse , and developing puppets for other family shows like The Very Hungry Crocodile and 101 Dalmatians musicals and The BFG at the Royal Shakespeare Company.

Christmas couldn’t get much better than sitting in a comfy chair and watching one of your favourite picture books live on stage! Don’t miss this festive fun for families: There’s a Bear on My Chair is at Queen Elizabeth Hall for a strictly limited season.

shows.name: English National Ballet: Romeo and Juliet
shows.description_short: Based on Shakespeare's ultimate tragedy, Rudolph Nureyev's stunning ballet to Prokofiev's score celebrates its 50th anniversary.
shows.typical_age_range: Best for age 10+. Under 5s not permitted. Children under 16 must be accompanied by and sat next to an adult.
shows.work_performed_name: English National Ballet: Romeo and Juliet
shows.description_full:
In January 2027, Romeo and Juliet, one of the greatest love stories ever told returns to the London Coliseum, just off Leicester Square, in a landmark revival from English National Ballet. In celebration of the ballet’s 50th anniversary, Rudolf Nureyev’s iconic production returns to the stage where it first premiered in 1977, for Queen Elizabeth II’s Silver Jubilee. Based on Shakespeare’s tragic romance, and set to a score by Sergei Prokofiev, “'Emotion pours from every corner of the [...] stage” (Bachtrack) - secure your tickets to English National Ballet: Romeo and Juliet today.

A balletic reimagining of Shakespeare’s ultimate tragedy, Romeo and Juliet is set in Renaissance Italy, in the divided city of Verona, where hostility between the Montagues and the Capulets threatens to erupt into violence. Amid simmering hatred and public clashes, fate intervenes when Romeo Montague and Juliet Capulet meet and fall instantly, irrevocably in love. Their relationship blooms in secret, but the world around them is ruled by loyalty, honour and revenge. As tensions escalate, their love is tested by forces beyond their control. The question at the heart of the story remains as urgent as ever: can young love survive in a society consumed by conflict?

Rudolf Nureyev’s choreography captures both the intimacy of first love and the electrifying drama of street battles and ballroom encounters. From the famous balcony pas de deux to the devastating final embrace, the ballet moves from tenderness to tragedy with gripping theatrical power.

Nureyev created the production in 1977 for Queen Elizabeth II’s Silver Jubilee, and it premiered at the London Coliseum the same year. Since its debut, Romeo and Juliet has become one of English National Ballet’s defining works, remaining a cornerstone of the company’s repertoire, admired for its scale, emotional clarity and faithful storytelling. Prokofiev’s score features some of the ballet's most instantly recognisable works and orchestral suites, including the stirring “Dance of the Knights”, performed live by the English National Ballet Philharmonic.

Now celebrating its 50th anniversary, this revival offers audiences the chance to see a landmark production in the theatre where it began, in a full-circle moment for one of ballet’s most enduring interpretations.

Casting varies by performance, with principal dancers from English National Ballet taking on the roles of Romeo, Juliet, Mercutio, Tybalt and the wider Verona ensemble.

The score is performed by the English National Ballet Philharmonic.

This production of English Romeo and Juliet was choreographed by Rudolf Nureyev(1938–1993). One of the most influential figures in 20th-century ballet, Nuruyev was a Soviet-born ballet dancer choreographer, and former director of the Paris Opera Ballet. Formerly a principal dancer for the Kirov (now Mariinsky) Ballet, Nureyev defected from the Soviet Union in 1961, establishing an international performing career which included roles with the Chicago Opera Ballet and as principal dancer for The Royal Ballet. As a choreographer, he is best known for his dramatically intense productions, which expanded roles for male dancers, including Swan Lake, The Sleeping Beauty, Don Quixote, and La Bayadère .

Sergei Prokofiev (1891–1953) was a Russian composer, pianist and conductor, regarded as one of the major composers of the 20th century. His body of work spans symphonies, concertos, operas, film scores and ballets. Among his best-known works are the ballet Cinderella , the children’s classic Peter and the Wolf , the opera War and Peace , and the cantata Alexander Nevsky (adapted from his score for Sergei Eisenstein’s film). His piano concertos and symphonies remain staples of the orchestral repertoire, and Prokofiev’s music continues to be widely performed worldwide.

You won’t want to miss this huge revival of one of literature's greatest romances, adapted into one of ballet’s most powerful works by one of the 20th century's most influential choreographers. Romeo and Juliet is “entertainment as vital, as alive and as stirring as can be” (The Telegraph), and returns to London to celebrate the production's 50th Anniversary. Allow yourself to fall in love - secure your tickets for English National Ballet: Romeo and Juliet at the London Coliseum today.

shows.name: Breaking The Waves
shows.description_short: Missy Mazzoli’s hauntingly beautiful operatic adaptation of the acclaimed film by Lars von Trier arrives at the London Coliseum in 2027.
shows.description_full:
Faith, heartbreak and loss combined with unflinching intensity are at the centre of Missy Mazzoli’s Breaking the Waves . A striking new production heads to the London Coliseum in 2027, a short walk from the National Gallery. This is your chance to experience one of the most contemporary operas of our time. Secure your tickets for this striking opera today!

Breaking the Waves is set in the Scottish Highlands in the early 1970s and centres on Bess McNeill and her husband, Jan. When her husband becomes paralysed in an offshore accident, the vows she made to Jan are put to the test. She is encouraged to have intimate relationships with other men and describe the experiences to him, convincing her that this will aid him.

The opera, which features a libretto by Royce Vavrek, is based on the 1996 film of the same name by Lars von Trier. It was adapted by Missy Mazzorli, one of the first two women who received a commission from the Metropolitan Opera House.

Staged in three acts, Breaking The Waves made its premiere in 2017 at the Skirball Center for the Performing Arts by Opera Philadelphia. It then went on to receive its European premiere in 2019 as part of a co-production with the Edinburgh International Festival and staged in association with Bristol Old Vic. The last time it was staged was at Detroit Opera in 2024.

The production won the inaugural Best New Opera Award from the Music Critics Association of North America and was also shortlisted for the Best World Premiere Prize at the International Opera Awards.

The principal cast includes:

Tinuke Craig will direct the production, conducted by Joana Carneiro.

Experience every beat of drama and emotion that Breaking the Waves has to offer for audiences. Book your tickets for a visceral, unflinching contemporary opera at the London Coliseum.

shows.name: Trial By Jury & The Zoo
shows.description_short: English National Opera presents a comic double bill of two satirical pieces by Gilbert & Sullivan, including an early Sullivan work.
shows.description_full:
A wildly entertaining double-bill of opera awaits you at the London Coliseum, a short walk from Trafalgar Square. Expect hilarity, an unexpected and heaps of chaos from Trial By Jury & The Zoo and experience a rare glimpse of Sullivan’s early work alongside a piece from one of opera’s most legendary partnerships for a limited time. Secure your tickets today!

The Zoo follows two young lovers who ultimately become engaged. Set in the gardens of a zoo, Thomas is concerned that Eliza, a refreshment seller, only cares about his money. Duke of Islington, a nobleman, is also in love with Eliza and, to win her affections, disguises himself as an ordinary man who works at the zoo. It’s a story of hidden identities, secret marriages and mischievous mix-ups that will have you laughing out loud.

Trial by Jury takes us to the courtroom, where Angelina is suing her former fiancé, Edwin, for breach of promise. What unfolds in this one-act opera is chaos, joyous absurdity, and an unexpected twist as the jury is easily swayed by emotion rather than the facts of the case. Trial by Jury is ultimately a sharp, humnourous satire of the legal system.

The Zoo is an opera by Gilbert and Rowe that made its premiere at the St James’s Theatre in London in 1875, before concluding its run at the Haymarket Theatre after a five-week run. There have been revivals of the piece in 1875 and 1879. The last time it was fully staged in London was at the Finborough Theatre in 2007.

Trial By Jury is an opera by Gilbert and Sullivan, one of opera’s most legendary partnerships. It initially ran for 131 performances in London, where it made its debut. After its original production in 1875, it has been revived multiple times. This will be the first time this production is staged at the ENO.

The cast for The Zoo :

Olivier Award winner Peter Whelan conducts both pieces. John Savournin directs, and the set and costume design is by takis.

Enjoy some comedic relief with a trip to see this operatic double-bill in the heart of London in 2027. The Trial by Jury & The Zoo are two sparky early works by Arthur Sullivan, jam-packed with wit, charm, and quick-fire humour. Book your tickets today!

shows.name: Ivanov
shows.description_short: Chris Pine makes his London stage debut in this contemporary reimagining of Chekhov's comic drama about the melancholic Nikolai Ivanov.
shows.description_full:
Hollywood superstar Chris Pine makes his London Theatre debut in Ivanov , the brand new adaptation from award-winning writer and director Simon Stone. This bold reimagining of Anton Chekhov's comic drama about the trials and tribulations of the melancholic Nikolai Ivanov comes to London for a strictly limited run, debuting at the Bridge Theatre just by the iconic Tower Bridge. Dont miss out - secure your tickets today!

Has Nikolai Ivanov ever been happy? Or has he just forgotten how? Despite his flourishing career and his loving marriage, Nikolai can’t help but feel like his world is falling apart. Could it be his ever-growing debts, the distaste of his parents-in-law, his relentless partying, or perhaps the mysterious illness plaguing his wife that is causing his melancholy? Or does he just need to get it together and rediscover his lust for life? When Nikolai heads to a party at his friend’s, the Lebedevs, it seems like the perfect chance to shake off his worries and get his groove back.

But as Nikolai stumbles from mishap to mishap, trying to put on a happy face, something unexpected causes his fortunes to begin looking up - an affair. In the fallout of his infidelity, can Nikolai turn his life around, save his marriage, fix his career and restore his will to live all in one fell swoop?

Ivanov was written in 1887, when Anton Chekov was commissioned to write a comedy for the Korsh Theatre, Moscow. Instead, Chekov wrote Ivanov, which was initially a drama, in only ten days. After the play’s debut, Chekhov undertook extensive rewrites, displeased with the work, and it later premiered fully in St. Petersburg in 1889, in its current dramatic-comedy form.

The play has since had many revivals, adaptations, translations and reinterpretations, including productions adapted by David Harrower for the Almeida and Chichester Festival Theatre, by David Hare for the Vivian Beaumont Theatre, New York, and by Tom Stoppard for Wyndham’s Theatre, which starred Kenneth Branagh. This bold new reimagining from writer-director Simon Stone explores all the highs and lows of Chekhov's original while firmly transplanting the action into modern day.

Chris Pine stars as Nikolai Ivanov. Pine is an Emmy-nominated American actor and director, best known for his roles as James T Kirk in the Star Trek reboot film series and as Steve Trevor in the Wonder Woman film franchise. His other credits include Don’t Worry Darling, Dungeons & Dragons: Honor Among Thieves, Just My Luck , and Poolman , which he co-wrote and directed. Ivanov marks Pine’s London theatre debut

Further casting is to be announced.

Ivanov is written and directed by Simon Stone, after Chekhov. Stone is an Olivier award-winning Australian writer, director and producer. Stone is best known for his bold, modern adaptations of classic plays. His adaptations include Phaedra for the National Theatre, Yerma starring Billie Piper, for the Young Vic, Medea for Ivo van Hove's company Internationaal Theater and The Lady From the Sea for the Bridge Theatre, starring Alicia Vikander.

Ivanov is presented by London Theatre Company in collaboration with Wouter van Ransbeek

Things might be rough for Nikolai Ivanov, but they’re certainly looking up for theatre goers, who get the chance see Hollywood royalty Chris Pine making his London stage debut! Secure your place today for this witty, keenly observed comic-drama from the award-winning team behind The Lady from The Sea - book tickets for Ivanov at the Bridge Theatre!
